component_identification.content: Core game includes a role deck and role tokens (16 roles/tokens in common base set), plus rules and often app-guided narration support. Setup uses exactly player-count + 3 role cards each game: one card dealt to each player and three face-down center cards. Not every role is used every session. Ensure selected role mix matches recommended setup charts; role balance depends on combination, not just count.

core_game_loop.content: Players receive secret roles and view only their own card. Night phase runs in fixed order as narrated by moderator/app; specific roles may view, swap, or alter cards. After night, all players discuss and deduce who is currently a werewolf based on claims and contradictions. Everyone votes simultaneously for one player. Resolve deaths and then determine which team wins using final role states, not original dealt roles.

detailed_rules.content: Choose role set appropriate to player count, shuffle, deal one per player plus three center cards. All players examine their own role card, then begin night with eyes closed. Narration order matters and must not be skipped: werewolf information steps first, then support/utility roles (e.g., seer viewing, robber swap-and-look, troublemaker swap-without-look, drunk center swap, insomniac final check), following whichever roles are in game. Players may gain information or alter role ownership during night; some roles act only if present. Morning discussion is open and often timed; players may bluff freely unless using no-lie variants. Voting is simultaneous. Player with most votes dies; ties can kill multiple players depending on exact vote distribution/rules text. Then evaluate win condition by final roles after all swaps. Village usually wins by eliminating at least one werewolf, or by correctly executing no-kill condition when no werewolf is in play; werewolf side wins otherwise. Special roles (e.g., Tanner, Hunter, Minion in relevant sets) can override baseline victory logic, so always check included-role text before final adjudication. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: One Night is a single-round social deduction game with team outcome, not cumulative points. End triggers immediately after vote resolution. Determine deaths, apply any death-trigger role effects, then check team victory conditions according to final role assignment. If using role-specific alternates (Tanner wants to die, etc.), apply those before announcing winners. Groups commonly play multiple rounds and rotate role sets; each round is self-contained.

beginner_strategy.content: In discussion, build a clear timeline of night actions and challenge inconsistencies in order of operation. Villagers should prioritize cross-confirmable claims rather than loud confidence alone. If you performed a swap role, disclose carefully—timing and target detail can prove authenticity. Werewolf-side players should prefer one coherent narrative over layered complicated lies. Coordinate final votes; scattered village votes frequently hand victory to werewolves. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text from one-night-ultimate-werewolf.txt as primary source. Reference snippet: CONTENTS: 16 role cards: 1 Doppelganger, 2 Werewolves, 1 Minion, 2 Masons, 1 Seer, 1 Robber, 1 Troublemaker, 1 Drunk, 1 Insomniac, 1 Hunter,
